Home

Readability Clean Article

Mozilla Readability is the algorithm Firefox Reader View uses to extract the primary article body from a web page. It favors long runs of paragraph text inside a containing element and penalizes navigation, advertising, and boilerplate markers.

This fixture exists precisely so that Readability can identify a single clean article body. The paragraphs are long enough to pass the length threshold Readability uses, and the surrounding chrome is minimal.

When an agent asks "what is this page about?", the answer should come from this article block and nothing else. A well-structured page makes that easy. A cluttered page makes it hard.

The clipper scoring engine uses Readability as a proxy signal: if Readability can extract a substantial fraction of the page as article text, the page is considered extractable by agents that rely on similar heuristics.

Why this matters

Agents that consume documentation often extract text before reasoning over it. If extraction fails, everything downstream fails. A page that scores well on Readability is one where the primary content is clearly demarcated and not buried under promotional chrome.

That separation is exactly what this fixture provides: a single article element, paragraphs of real length, and essentially no competing content elsewhere in the document.
